title: Export an SPSS / CSV / Excel file to a labeled Excel workbook
summary: Writes a two-sheet Excel workbook: sheet 1 contains the data with value labels applied, sheet 2 contains variable metadata (name, label, type, measure, missing codes).
description: Input format is inferred from the file extension: .sav/.zsav are read via readstat; .csv/.tsv are read as delimited text (UTF-8 with BOM tolerated); .xlsx files are read from the first sheet. The exported workbook preserves SPSS variable labels and value labels where present; categorical integer codes are rendered with their labels applied while the raw codes are retained in a hidden companion column so the workbook round-trips through `write-sav` (with from_export_excel=true) without information loss.

arguments.name: output_dir
arguments.description: Directory for the output .xlsx; created if missing.

notes: Round-trip: an export-excel workbook can be fed back to `write-sav` with `from_export_excel = true` to reconstruct the .sav from the workbook's embedded metadata.
CSV inputs have no SPSS metadata, so the variable-metadata sheet is populated from detected types only.

artifacts.title: Labeled Excel export
artifacts.description: A two-sheet Excel workbook. Sheet 1 contains the data with value labels applied (integer codes replaced by text labels). Sheet 2 lists variable metadata (name, label, storage type, measure level, missing codes). Use this to share data with stakeholders who do not have SPSS.

details:
The output workbook has two sheets. Sheet 1 contains the data: each row is one respondent, each column is one variable. For categorical variables (those with SPSS value labels), the cell shows the label text rather than the raw integer code. Sheet 2 contains variable metadata: one row per variable with name, label, storage type, SPSS measure level, and declared missing-value codes.

To preserve round-trip fidelity, each categorical column in Sheet 1 has a hidden companion column that stores the raw numeric codes. When the workbook is fed back to `write-sav` with `from_export_excel = true`, those hidden columns are used to reconstruct the original integer codes without loss. This means you can share the workbook, receive it back with labelling edits (on Sheet 2), and reconstruct a corrected .sav.

CSV inputs have no SPSS metadata: Sheet 2 is populated with detected types only and there are no value labels or missing codes.

see_also.description: Round-trip: read back an export-excel workbook and reconstruct a .sav with `from_export_excel = true`.
A lighter alternative that outputs a dictionary workbook — without the full data rows — for metadata inspection.

examples.title: Export an SPSS file to a labeled Excel workbook
